title: NIS2 SCADA System Access Monitoring
exclusions: Does not determine legal applicability, complete regulator submissions, approve policy decisions, or replace counsel or competent-authority review. Does not certify functional safety of the controller—only visibility of access telemetry available to Splunk.
evidence: Scheduled saved search `UC-22.2.37`, dashboard panels on restricted NIS2 posture workbook, and archived exports in `index=audit_evidence` retained per your records policy.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(a) requires information system security policies. The UC monitors interactive and remote access to SCADA/HMI systems from privileged session logs and firewall telemetry. It proves access is logged and anomalies are surfaced but cannot assess whether the access policy itself is proportionate to the OT risk, hence partial.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(a) (Risk analysis and information-system security policies) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.37: NIS2 SCADA System Access Monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.37' running on WinEventLog:Security EventCode=4624/4648, cyberark:session, nis2_scada_maintenance_windows.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: A contractor account starts a CyberArk session to asset HEX-PLANT-01 while `nis2_scada_assets.csv` lists only internal engineers for that asset_id; the saved search returns a row with nonzero sessions and actions including modify within a closed maintenance window.
controlTest.negativeScenario: An operator listed in `authorized_users` performs routine read-only polling during an open maintenance window with matching `window_start/window_end`; after roster and window enrichment the search returns zero notable rows for that session.

dataSources: `index=ot_access` with `sourcetype=scada:access_log` (operator logins, writes, engineering actions; extracted fields: `asset_id`, `access_user`, `session_id`, `action`, `result`). `index=pam` with `sourcetype=cyberark:session` (privileged sessions into OT; fields: `user`, `target_host`, `protocol`). Splunk Add-on for CyberArk ([Splunkbase 2891](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2891)) and Splunk Enterprise Security ([Splunkbase 263](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/263)). KV/CSV lookup `nis2_scada_assets.csv` with columns `asset_id`, `asset_name`, `scada_type`, `criticality`, `authorized_users`. Optional CIM Authentication tagging for identity analytics.
app: Splunk Add-on for CyberArk (Splunkbase 2891), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263)
spl:
(index=ot_access sourcetype=scada:access_log) OR (index=pam sourcetype=cyberark:session) earliest=-24h
| eval asset_id=coalesce(asset_id, plant_asset_id, target_host, dest_host, TargetAddress)
| eval access_user=coalesce(access_user, user, User, user_name)
| lookup nis2_scada_assets.csv asset_id OUTPUT asset_name, scada_type, criticality, authorized_users
| where isnotnull(scada_type) AND criticality IN ("high","critical")
| eval action_norm=lower(coalesce(action, activity, EventType, "login"))
| eval user_in_roster=if(match(lower(authorized_users), lower(access_user)), 1, 0)
| where user_in_roster=0 OR match(action_norm, "(write|modify|download|program|force)")
| lookup nis2_scada_maintenance_windows.csv asset_id OUTPUT start, end
| eval starttime=strptime(start,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") | eval endtime=strptime(end,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval in_maint=if(_time>=starttime AND _time<=endtime,1,0)
| where in_maint=0 OR user_in_roster=0
| stats earliest(_time) as first_seen latest(_time) as last_seen values(action_norm) as actions dc(session_id) as sessions by asset_id asset_name scada_type access_user criticality
| sort - sessions
description: Detects logins, privileged sessions, and high-risk engineering actions against inventoried plant control assets when the actor is outside the approved user roster for that asset, or when the activity occurs outside an approved maintenance interval.
value: Turns scattered plant-access logs and vault sessions into defensible proof that only expected people touched critical control gear at expected times, so leadership can answer oversight questions without assembling spreadsheets after the fact.
implementation: Ingest `scada:access_log` into `index=ot_access` and CyberArk sessions into `index=pam` using Splunkbase 2891. Maintain `nis2_scada_assets.csv` and `nis2_scada_maintenance_windows.csv` under change control. Map fields `asset_id`, `access_user`, `action`, `session_id`. Schedule the saved search every 15 minutes over rolling 24 hours; route results to ES correlation or a compliance dashboard. Normalize identities toward the CIM Authentication model where possible for ES identity tables.
visualization: Table of asset_id, asset_name, access_user, actions, sessions; timeline of first_seen/last_seen; single value of distinct off-roster users; map or bar split by scada_type. Optional ES Incident Review drilldown when Splunkbase 263 is licensed.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication where nodename=Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable for Authentication when ES is deployed; summary range 30 days minimum for roster analytics.
knownFalsePositives:
• **Break-glass CyberArk sessions** — Distinguish: ticketed emergency change with duty manager approval in ServiceNow. Suppress: time-bound rows in `nis2_scada_breakglass_exceptions.csv` keyed on `access_user`, `asset_id`, and expiry `_time`.
• **New hire or rotated contractor identity** — Distinguish: HR effective-dated roster in workday export matches `access_user` but lookup stale. Suppress: refresh `nis2_scada_assets.csv` same-day; short filter `where effective_date<=now()` after lookup enrichment.
• **Asset_id remapping after line shutdown** — Distinguish: old `asset_id` still appears on legacy HMI logs while CMDB shows merged tag. Suppress: maintain `nis2_scada_asset_aliases.csv` lookup mapping retired IDs to canonical `asset_id` before the roster check.
• **Vendor jump-server shared account** — Distinguish: single service account used by multiple named engineers; logs lack human name. Suppress: exception register column `shared_account_override` with mandatory ticket reference; join `cyberark:session` ClientHost before filtering.
• **Read-only monitoring poll mistaken as access** — Distinguish: `action` contains poll/heartbeat from IT monitoring; source subnet documented. Suppress: `where NOT cidrmatch("10.40.0.0/16", src_ip)` or dedicated allow list lookup `nis2_scada_monitoring_subnets.csv`.
equipmentModels: CyberArk Privileged Access Security (PAM for SCADA jump-host session logging)
Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, change_request for SCADA maintenance windows)
Splunk Edge Hub (Modbus TCP/OPC-UA data from SCADA HMI/PLC network)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, Authentication data model)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `ot_access`, `pam` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `nis2_scada_assets.csv`, `nis2_scada_maintenance_windows.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('nis2_scada_assets.csv','nis2_scada_maintenance_windows.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.21(2)(a)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(a) (Risk analysis and information-system security policies) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.37: NIS2 SCADA System Access Monitoring." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `ot_access`, `pam` under `scada:access_log`, `cyberark:session`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


One operational consideration shapes the deployment of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Ingest `scada:access_log` into `index=ot_access` and CyberArk sessions into `index=pam` using Splunkbase 2891. Maintain `nis2_scada_assets.csv` and `nis2_scada_maintenance_windows.csv` under change control. Map fields `asset_id`, `access_user`, `action`, `session_id`. Schedule the saved search every 15 minutes over rolling 24 hours; route results to ES correlation or a compliance dashboard. Normalize identities toward the CIM Authentication model where possible for ES identity tables. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "NIS2 SCADA System Access Monitoring," the aggregation summarises access activity — the control is about WHO accessed WHAT, so the key fields in the aggregate map directly to 'principal' and 'resource' for the compliance narrative.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `nis2_scada_assets.csv` — the asset inventory register that maps hosts/IPs to owners and criticality. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`asset_name, scada_type, criticality, authorized_users`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `asset_id`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `nis2_scada_assets.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval asset_id = lower(asset_id)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 24 hours.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Authentication` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication where nodename=Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Authentication` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.37: NIS2 SCADA System Access Monitoring]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.37,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=ot_access sourcetype=scada:access_log earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `asset_id`, `access_user`, `action_norm`, `scada_type`, `authorized_users`. Run:

```spl
index=ot_access sourcetype=scada:access_log earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table asset_id, access_user, action_norm, scada_type, authorized_users
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `nis2_scada_assets.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup nis2_scada_assets.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.37: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Asset Id (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Asset Name (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Access User (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 4 — Actions (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 5 — Sessions; Timeline Of First Seen/Last Seen; Single Value Of Distinct Off-Roster Users; Map Or Bar Split By Scada Type. Optional Es Incident Review Drilldown When Splunkbase 263 Is Licensed (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: We watch who connects to the computers that run important plants and factories. When someone shows up who should not be there, or they poke around outside an agreed maintenance window, we highlight it so leaders can ask good questions before harm spreads.
